dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for diabetic hyperglycemia.
Hi, I'm Brad, an EMT with the rescue squad. My partner is Julie. What made you call 911 today?
I've been checking my blood sugar and it's really high. I'm also having trouble breathing.
Mr. Smith, can you open your eyes for me?
You opened your eyes spontaneously, which is great. Can you tell me your name?
James, but everyone calls me Jim.
Thanks, Jim. What city are we in now?
Richmond.
Good. What day is today?
Saturday.
Jim, can you squeeze my fingers for me?
(squeezes fingers firmly)
Nice and strong. Thank you, Jim.
Jim, can I check your breathing with the oxygen you're on? Is that okay with you?
Sure, no problem.
Your oxygen appears to be working. Are you using your usual setting?
Yeah, 2 liters per minute.
Jim, we're going to take your breathing rate now.
You're taking about 26 breaths per minute right now.
Jim, we are checking your oxygen saturation now with a clip on your finger. It's 86% right now.
Your lungs sound clear, and I don't see any signs of obstruction.
Jim, how are you doing with your breathing now?
It feels a bit tight, but I've been feeling it like this for a while now.
Do you think you might be struggling more if your oxygen were off?
Yeah, I think I would.
Now we'll check your blood pressure. Let me take a second to do that.
Blood pressure is 205 over 95.
And your pulse is 121 beats per minute.
Your skin is warm to the touch, pulse is strong, and I don't feel any swelling or edema.
Do you have any chest pain, dizziness, sweating, or weakness?
No, but I did have a fever earlier in the week.
Any coughing, nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea?
No, just short on air and a bit fatigued.
Jim, can you tell me about your breathing and the high blood sugar?

I came home from the hospital yesterday because I had a lung infection after my prior COVID illness. When I checked my sugar it was really high and I figured I'd better get someone to take a look at it.
It sounds like you took your medications as normal - did you?
Yeah, I only missed one dose of metformin during the hospitalization.
Good to hear. Is there any unusual food or drinks that you might have had recently?
I've been eating the Dole peaches a lot recently.
Jim, what's your medical history?
I have COPD, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and type 2 diabetes. I've had prior issues with lung infections.
What allergies do you have, Jim?
I'm allergic to ibuprofen, specifically Motrin.
Jim, what medications are you currently taking?
I'm on amlodipine, metformin (though I skipped one dose), and albuterol.
When was your last meal?
I had a light lunch about four hours ago.
What happened leading up to today's call?
I was just released from Chippenham Hospital. I had an infection in my lungs from my previous cold.
Jim, we need you to sign a consent form so we can treat and transport you. Okay?
Yes, I understand.
Jim, how would you rate your level of trouble breathing on a scale of 0 to 10 if we consider breathing as "pain"?
It's about a 0 in terms of chest pain, but an 8 for breathing.
Skin is pink, warm, and dry, with no visible injuries or rashes.
You're on home oxygen, so I don't need to do a separate examination of your airway. We continue to monitor as needed.
Can you take a deep breath for me so I can listen to your lungs?
Jim, we're going to check a 12-lead EKG now.
Here we are. The EKG shows sinus tachycardia with some PVCs and no dysrhythmias.
No ST segment changes. Results of 4 lead monitoring came in during the primary assessment.
Any abdominal pain or discomfort, Jim? Have you noticed anything related to digestion?
No discomfort there, and I haven't had diarrhea or other stomach issues.
Jim, we are following the diabetic hyperglycemia protocol. That involves checking your blood sugar and IV fluids for hydration.
We're going to check your blood sugar now. You may feel a slight pinch.
Blood sugar is 535 mg/dL, very high.
If you're okay with it, we'd like to give you a higher flow of oxygen and start an IV fluid.
Jim, we found a vein in your arm and start an IV with normal saline to hydrate you.
Oxygen is set to 6 liters per minute via nasal cannula.
You're going to get 750 mL of fluids. You may feel some pressure.
Blood glucose remains unchanged since our last check at 535.
Blood pressure is now 152 over 84, and pulse is 113 beats per minute.
SpO2 is 95 now, and breathing has slowed to 18 per minute.
Your EKG still shows tachycardia and PVCs, but no ST segment changes so far.
Jim, we need to move you now to the stretcher. Would you be able to walk with my help?
We'll assist you with the ambulation from your chair to the stretcher.
Jim, you'll be going to Retreat Hospital for care.
Jim is now secured on the stretcher. He's alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation-A&O x4-with a GCS of 15 (E4, V5, M6).
No signs of AMS noted, and he remains responsive throughout handling.
SpO2 improved with O2 and trending better, respirations dropped from 26 to 16 per minute.
We've placed you on the stretcher now. You're all set for transport to Retreat Hospital for specialist evaluation of your hyperglycemia and mild shortness of breath.
Final vitals read: 125 over 77, pulse of 107, and SpO2 at 96. He appears improved.
Blood sugar remains at 535, but we are on continuous fluids to lower it.
Mr. Smith, you're going to Retreat because their doctors are familiar with treating hyperglycemia.
On your go, for a 6-liter nasal cannula setting and 750 mL IV normal saline started at 20:04. All secondary assessments conclude with blood sugar unchanged and no new issues.
I'll make sure you're properly buckled in and monitored en route.
Jim, any change in how you're feeling now after the fluids and oxygen?
Still high blood sugar, but I feel less short of breath.
You also have a history of high cholesterol. Let me make note of that for the hospital.
Please don't take ibuprofen; you're allergic to it.
Your airway and breathing are patent, and there's no cyanosis.
Jim, are you able to tell me what day it is?
It's Saturday, like I said before.
Great. You're awake, alert, and oriented.
Jim, just for clarification, your breathing rate is now 16 per minute.
Mr. Smith, you're going to Retreat because their doctors are familiar with treating hyperglycemia.
Jim, I just gave another round of IV fluids.
Jim, I just repositioned the IV line - everything looks good.
Any change in your level of breath discomfort since we placed you on the stretcher?
Still 8, but not quite as overwhelming.
Your IV is running smoothly now. We should expect a drop in glucose soon.
Rechecking your temperature-it's 97.8 in the ear, so no fever noted at this moment.
You'll be receiving fluids until you see a doctor, so keep hydrated.
I'll monitor your IV and O2 units so there's no blockage during transport.
Let's start this transport now. Everything's set with the hospital on standby.
